text: What is a monosaccharide?
choices: A polymer made of many amino acids
A simple sugar that can serve as a building block for larger carbohydrates
A lipid made of glycerol and fatty acids
A nucleotide found only in RNA
explain: Monosaccharides are single sugar molecules that serve as the building blocks of larger carbohydrates.

text: What is a polysaccharide?
choices: A large carbohydrate made of many monosaccharides linked together
A protein composed of many amino acids
A lipid with three fatty acid tails
A single sugar molecule used only in RNA
explain: Polysaccharides are long carbohydrate polymers built from monosaccharide units.

text: What is a glycosidic linkage?
choices: A bond that links amino acids in a protein
A bond that links monosaccharides in carbohydrates
A bond that links nucleotides in DNA
A weak interaction between two water molecules
explain: Glycosidic linkages are the covalent bonds that join sugar monomers in carbohydrates.

text: Which part of a phospholipid is hydrophobic?
choices: The phosphate head
The fatty acid tails
The glycerol only
The entire molecule is equally hydrophilic
explain: The hydrocarbon fatty acid tails are nonpolar and repel water, making them hydrophobic.

text: Which interaction is especially important in stabilizing the tertiary structure of some proteins through covalent bonding?
choices: Hydrogen bonds
Ionic interactions
Disulfide bonds
Hydrophobic interactions
explain: Disulfide bonds are covalent links between cysteine residues that can stabilize tertiary structure.

text: What does it mean for a molecule to be amphipathic?
choices: It has only acidic properties
It has both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions
It contains both DNA and RNA
It can act as both a protein and a lipid
explain: Amphipathic molecules have both polar (hydrophilic) and nonpolar (hydrophobic) regions.

text: Which sugar is found in DNA?
choices: Ribose
Deoxyribose
Glucose
Fructose
explain: DNA contains deoxyribose as its pentose sugar, while RNA contains ribose.

text: Which statement best defines a peptide bond?
choices: A bond between two fatty acids
A bond between two amino acids formed during dehydration synthesis
A bond between two nucleotides in DNA
A weak attraction between two nonpolar molecules
explain: Peptide bonds covalently link amino acids together during dehydration synthesis to build proteins.

text: Which macromolecule includes enzymes?
choices: Carbohydrates
Lipids
Proteins
Nucleic acids
explain: Most enzymes are proteins that catalyze biochemical reactions.

text: What is the main reason pH matters in biological systems?
choices: pH can affect the charge and shape of molecules such as proteins
pH determines whether cells contain carbon
pH changes convert proteins directly into lipids
pH only matters outside cells, not inside them
explain: pH influences protonation states and charges of side chains, which can alter folding and function of biomolecules.

text: A researcher finds that an unknown polymer is made of repeating glucose monomers. The polymer can be rapidly broken down by animal digestive enzymes. Which conclusion is most likely?
choices: The polymer is likely a storage carbohydrate with bond types recognized by animal enzymes
The polymer must be DNA because it is made of repeating subunits
The polymer is definitely cellulose because all glucose polymers are digested equally
The polymer must be a lipid because it stores energy
explain: Digestibility depends on linkage type; storage carbohydrates (with appropriate linkages) are often digestible by animals.

text: A protein contains a cysteine residue that forms a covalent bond with another cysteine elsewhere in the polypeptide. What is the most likely effect?
choices: It may help stabilize the protein’s three-dimensional shape
It will always break the protein into two pieces
It converts the protein into RNA
It removes the primary structure of the protein
explain: Disulfide bonds (covalent links between cysteines) can reinforce tertiary structure and protein stability.

text: A scientist replaces the phosphate-containing head of a phospholipid with a fully nonpolar group. Which outcome is most likely?
choices: The molecule would be less able to interact with water and less suited for typical membrane organization
The molecule would become more soluble in water than before
The molecule would automatically become a protein
The fatty acid tails would become charged
explain: Removing the polar head reduces hydrophilicity and interferes with membrane self-assembly in aqueous environments.

text: A student says, “Because both DNA and proteins are polymers, changing one monomer should never have a major effect.” Which response is best?
choices: The student is correct because polymers are always tolerant of monomer changes
The student is incorrect because one changed monomer can alter sequence, structure, and function
The student is incorrect because proteins are not polymers
The student is correct because only lipids are affected by monomer changes
explain: A single monomer change can alter primary sequence and cascade to affect higher-order structure and function.

text: An aqueous solution contains a molecule that has one positively charged end and one negatively charged end. Which property is most likely?
choices: The molecule is nonpolar and will avoid water
The molecule can form favorable interactions with water because of its polarity
The molecule must be a saturated fatty acid
The molecule cannot participate in hydrogen bonding
explain: Opposite charges on different ends indicate polarity, which promotes interactions with water and possible hydrogen bonding.

text: A researcher compares a normal enzyme to a mutant enzyme. The mutant enzyme has the same primary structure length but one amino acid change near the active site. Which is the best prediction?
choices: The mutant enzyme may bind the substrate less effectively because active-site chemistry can be altered
The mutant enzyme will necessarily be longer
The mutant enzyme must become a carbohydrate
The mutant enzyme can no longer contain peptide bonds
explain: A substitution near the active site can alter binding interactions and reduce catalytic efficiency.

text: Two RNA molecules have different nucleotide sequences but similar overall functions. Which statement best explains how both can still function?
choices: Nucleotide sequence never matters in RNA
Different sequences can sometimes still fold into structures that support similar roles
RNA functions only if it is double-stranded like DNA
RNA is made of amino acids, so nucleotide sequence is irrelevant
explain: Function often depends on 3D structure; different sequences can fold into similar structural motifs supporting similar functions.

text: A scientist is comparing a triglyceride and a phospholipid. Which feature best distinguishes the phospholipid?
choices: It has three fatty acid tails and no polar region
It has a significant polar region and is especially useful in membrane structure
It is made of amino acids instead of fatty acids
It stores genetic information
explain: Phospholipids have a polar headgroup and nonpolar tails, enabling membrane formation; triglycerides lack the polar head.

text: Why can a buffer help maintain homeostasis in organisms?
choices: It resists drastic pH changes that could disrupt biological molecules
It permanently locks pH at exactly 7.0 in all situations
It removes all acids and bases from the system
It converts hydrogen ions into carbon atoms
explain: Buffers minimize pH changes by absorbing or releasing protons, helping preserve conditions required for biological function.

text: A mutation changes a hydrophilic amino acid on the outside of a cytoplasmic protein to a strongly hydrophobic amino acid. Which effect is most likely?
choices: The protein could become less stable in the watery cytoplasmic environment
The mutation will guarantee the protein becomes a membrane phospholipid
The mutation will remove all amino acids from the protein surface
The protein will definitely become longer
explain: Exposed hydrophobic residues can destabilize soluble proteins by disrupting favorable interactions with water.

text:
Students perform Benedict’s test on four samples after heating:
Tube 1: blue remains blue
Tube 2: blue turns green
Tube 3: blue turns orange
Tube 4: blue turns brick red

Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Tube 4 likely has the highest concentration of reducing sugar among the samples
Tube 1 has the highest reducing sugar concentration because it stayed blue
Tube 2 contains only proteins because it turned green
Tube 3 must contain lipids because it changed color
explain: Benedict’s reagent changes toward orange/red with increasing concentrations of reducing sugars; brick red indicates the most reducing sugar.
experiment: Four samples were tested with Benedict's reagent and heated; color intensity was used as a semi-quantitative indicator of reducing sugar concentration.

text: A class performs Biuret tests on several samples. Sample Q turns violet, while Sample R remains blue. Which interpretation is most reasonable?
choices: Sample Q likely contains peptide bonds, while Sample R likely contains little to no protein
Sample Q contains only lipids, while Sample R contains only nucleic acids
Sample R contains more protein because it stayed blue
Sample Q must contain DNA because it changed color
explain: Biuret reagent produces a violet color in the presence of peptide bonds (proteins); blue indicates no strong presence of protein.
experiment: Samples were tested with Biuret reagent and observed for color change; violet indicates peptide bonds.

text: Researchers compare two solutions of the same protein. Solution 1 is kept at pH 7. Solution 2 is adjusted to pH 2. The protein in Solution 2 loses function and forms clumps. Which explanation is best supported?
choices: The extreme pH likely altered side-chain charges and disrupted the interactions that maintain protein shape
The protein in Solution 2 must have gained extra amino acids
The protein in Solution 2 changed its DNA sequence
The protein in Solution 2 turned into a carbohydrate because of the acid
explain: Extreme pH changes protonation states of side chains, disrupting ionic and hydrogen-bonding interactions and causing denaturation and aggregation.
experiment: The protein was incubated at different pH values and then assayed for activity and solubility; the low-pH sample lost function and aggregated.

text: A mutation changes one nucleotide in a DNA coding region. The resulting protein differs by one amino acid and folds abnormally. Which statement best links the mutation to the phenotype?
choices: A small change in primary structure can alter higher levels of protein structure and therefore function
DNA mutations affect only nucleic acids and never proteins
The mutation must have removed all hydrogen bonds in the organism
The abnormal folding proves the protein was originally a lipid
explain: A single amino acid substitution can change folding and disrupt function, linking the DNA mutation to the observed phenotype.
experiment: A single-nucleotide substitution was introduced and the resulting protein's folding and activity were compared to wild type; the mutant folded abnormally.

text: Students compare how two unknown molecules behave in water. Molecule X disperses uniformly. Molecule Y forms droplets and separates. Additional testing shows Molecule Y is made mostly of long hydrocarbon chains. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Molecule Y is likely hydrophobic because hydrocarbon chains are largely nonpolar
Molecule X must be a lipid because it disperses in water
Molecule Y is likely highly polar because it forms droplets
Molecule X and Y must be chemically identical because both contain carbon
explain: Long hydrocarbon chains are nonpolar and do not interact well with water, causing droplet formation and phase separation.
experiment: Two unknown samples were added to water and observed for dispersion; composition analysis showed hydrocarbon-rich Molecule Y.

text:
A teacher provides students with three unknown samples:

Sample A:
- Positive Benedict’s test
- Negative Biuret test

Sample B:
- Negative Benedict’s test
- Positive Biuret test

Sample C:
- Negative Benedict’s test
- Negative Biuret test
- Oily texture and translucent spot on paper

Which identification is most likely?
choices: A = reducing sugar, B = protein, C = lipid
A = protein, B = lipid, C = nucleic acid
A = lipid, B = reducing sugar, C = protein
A = starch, B = water, C = amino acid
explain: Benedict's positive indicates reducing sugar; Biuret positive indicates protein; oily texture and translucent spot indicate lipid.
experiment: Three samples were tested with Benedict's and Biuret reagents and observed for physical properties (translucent spot on paper).

text: A scientist designs an experiment to compare membrane-forming ability of two lipids in water. Lipid 1 forms stable bilayer-like structures. Lipid 2 aggregates into droplets instead. Which structural difference most likely explains the result?
choices: Lipid 1 is amphipathic, while Lipid 2 is mostly nonpolar
Lipid 1 contains no carbon, while Lipid 2 does
Lipid 2 must be made of amino acids
Lipid 1 has no hydrophilic region at all
explain: Amphipathic molecules with both polar heads and nonpolar tails form bilayers; nonpolar lipids tend to form droplets.
experiment: Two lipids were added to water and their self-assembled structures were observed under microscopy; Lipid 1 formed bilayers, Lipid 2 formed droplets.

text: Students test the effect of temperature on two samples of the same enzyme. Sample A is incubated at 25°C. Sample B is incubated at 75°C for several minutes, then both are returned to 25°C and tested. Sample B shows very low activity. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: High temperature likely disrupted the enzyme’s structure in a way that reduced later activity
The enzyme in Sample B gained extra nucleotides
Sample B now contains more peptide bonds than Sample A
The enzyme in Sample B must have been converted into a phospholipid
explain: Heat can denature proteins by disrupting noncovalent interactions, causing loss of activity even after cooling.
experiment: Two enzyme samples were incubated at different temperatures, returned to assay conditions, and activity was measured; the high-temperature sample lost activity.
